summary: Dan Shapiro recounts how he casually asked Codex CLI to install Steve Yegge's Beads tool on Windows, only to discover it had no Windows support—and Codex autonomously ported it instead. He contrasts this with a 2023 BabyAGI disaster where an agent burned $122 in API calls trying to compile GCC from source just to recommend a standing desk. The juxtaposition illustrates how far AI agents have come in two years: from expensive recursive spirals to genuinely useful autonomous problem-solving. He notes Codex CLI's advantage of running on a flat subscription (no surprise bills) and its ability to stay on task, while acknowledging Claude Code has better tooling but GPT-5-codex is more focused.
key_points: AI agents have evolved dramatically from 2023's BabyAGI (which recursed into absurdity) to 2025's Codex CLI (which autonomously ports software)
Codex CLI independently recognized Beads lacked Windows support and created a working port without being asked, submitting an actual PR
Flat-rate subscription models for AI agents eliminate the runaway-cost problem that plagued early autonomous agents
Shapiro draws a practical distinction between Claude Code (better tooling) and GPT-5-codex (better focus and task completion), using both
money_quotes: I didn't read the compatibility notes. I didn't check to see if it had Windows support. I needed coffee.
I did not get a standing desk recommendation. I got a $122 lesson in why you don't let agents recurse unsupervised.

When I finally killed the process, it had burned through $122.47 in OpenAI API calls and was halfway through cloning the GCC source repository so it could build a compiler for Windows. To audit a research paper. To recommend a desk.
What I was not expecting was to discover that there was no Windows version and it had ported it itself.
Who needs to remember bash or powershell when you can use english?
The tooling is so much better in Claude Code, but gpt-5-codex on high thinking is just devastatingly effective.
Sonnet has the 'oh squirrel' tendency to do what it thinks you should want, rather than what you asked (pleaded, begged) for.
key_insight: The gap between AI agents that spiral into expensive absurdity and ones that autonomously solve real problems has closed in just two years, turning accidental software ports into casual Tuesday mornings.
